perturbation.original_text: 10.2. Product Warranty by IAC. IAC hereby represents and warrants that for a period of [*] after the Manufacturing Date (the "Warranty Period"), a Product Unit will be free from defects in manufacturing process and defects in workmanship, will conform to general expectations of performance of wireless audio products and will conform to the Statement of Work for the applicable Product.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.2. Product Warranty by IAC. IAC hereby represents and warrants that for a period of [*] after the Manufacturing Date (the "Warranty Period"), a Product Unit will be free from defects in manufacturing process and defects in workmanship, will conform to minimum expectations of performance of wireless audio products and will conform to the Statement of Work for the applicable Product.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'general' to 'minimum' weakens the warranty, potentially contradicting consumer protection laws that imply a standard of reasonable performance.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 2304 - Federal Minimum Standards for Warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act sets minimum standards for warranties on consumer products. By stating the product will only conform to 'minimum expectations', the contract may fail to meet the Act's requirement for a clear and understandable warranty, potentially reducing the manufacturer's obligations below what is legally required for a 'full' warranty. This creates uncertainty regarding the enforceability of the warranty.

Definitions. 2302. Rules governing contents of warranties. 2303. Designation of written warranties. 2304. Federal minimum standards for warranties. 2305. Full and limited warranting of a consumer product. 2306. Service contracts; rules for full, clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions; addition to or in lieu of written warranty. 2307. Designation of representatives by warrantor to perform duties under written or implied warranty. 2308. Implied warranties. 2309. Procedures applicable to promulgation of rules by Commission. 2310. Remedies in consumer disputes. 2311. Applicability to other laws. 2312. Effective dates. §2301. Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a supplier to a buyer which relates to the nature of the material or workmanship and affir
perturbation.justification: The change from 'general expectations' to 'minimum expectations' weakens the product warranty provided by IAC. The Law Explanation states that the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act requires a 'clear and understandable warranty', and setting a standard of merely 'minimum expectations' may not meet this requirement. Snippet 2 references 15 USC Ch. 50, which governs consumer product warranties and sets federal minimum standards. By explicitly lowering the warranty standard to 'minimum', the contract introduces language that could be interpreted as not meeting the federal minimum standards required for warranties, creating a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition as it deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 4.8. Compliance with Laws and Regulations for Manufacturing; Fair Labor Practices. IAC shall comply with all applicable laws and regulations related to the manufacturing and/or production of the Products in jurisdictions in which IAC manufactures the Products, including labor and employment, environmental, safety, tax and other similar regulations. In addition, IAC currently complies and will continue to comply with any applicable regulations regarding foreign child labor laws and other abusive labor practices.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.8. Compliance with Laws and Regulations for Manufacturing; Fair Labor Practices. IAC shall comply with most applicable laws and regulations related to the manufacturing and/or production of the Products in jurisdictions in which IAC manufactures the Products, including labor and employment, environmental, safety, tax and other similar regulations. In addition, IAC currently complies and will continue to comply with some applicable regulations regarding foreign child labor laws and other abusive labor practices.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'all' to 'most' and 'any' to 'some' introduces ambiguity regarding which laws IAC is obligated to follow, creating a potential conflict with mandatory legal requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FLSA mandates compliance with minimum wage and overtime pay requirements. By stating that IAC will only comply with 'most applicable laws,' the contract suggests that IAC might not fully adhere to FLSA requirements, directly contradicting the law and creating a loophole for non-compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: in the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA). The federal minimum wage is $7.25 per hour effective July 24, 2009. Many states also have minimum wage laws. Some state laws provide greater employee protections; employers must comply with both. The FLSA does not provide wage payment collection procedures for an employee’s usual or promised wages or commissions in excess of those required by the FLSA. However, some states do have laws under which such claims (sometimes including fringe benefits) may be filed. perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'all' with 'most' and 'any' with 'some,' which directly contradicts the principle of full compliance with applicable laws, particularly those related to labor practices as suggested by the Law Explanation's reference to FLSA compliance for minimum wage and overtime. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 specifically mentions the federal minimum wage as part of the FLSA, the core contradiction lies in the agreement's suggestion that full adherence to all relevant regulations may not be guaranteed. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by noting the ambiguity introduced which creates a potential conflict with mandatory legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: 16.10. Dispute Settlement; Governing Law. Any dispute or claim arising out of or in relation to this Agreement, or the interpretation, making, performance, breach or termination thereof, shall first be referred to the responsible executives of each party, each of whom shall use their best reasonable efforts in good faith to reach a mutually agreeable solution. If the parties are unable to resolve the dispute or claim despite such efforts, the dispute or claim shall be settled by binding arbitration under the International Rules of the American Arbitration Association as presently in force ("Rules") and by three (3) arbitrators appointed in accordance with such Rules. Judgment on the award rendered may be entered in any court having jurisdiction thereof. The place of arbitration shall be Los Angeles, California USA. Any monetary award shall be in U.S. dollars and the arbitration shall be conducted in the English language. The parties may apply to any court of competent jurisdiction for temporary or permanent injunctive relief, without breach of this Section 16.10 and without any abridgment of the powers of the arbitrator. This Agreement shall be governed by the law of California, U.S.A. and the arbitrators shall apply California law to the merits of any dispute or claim, without reference to conflict of law principles. The arbitration proceedings shall be governed by federal arbitration law and by the Rules, without reference to state arbitration law. The parties hereby exclude the application of the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods
perturbation.changed_text: 16.10. Dispute Settlement; Governing Law. Any dispute or claim arising out of or in relation to this Agreement, or the interpretation, making, performance, breach or termination thereof, shall first be referred to the responsible executives of each party, each of whom shall use their best reasonable efforts in bad faith to reach a mutually agreeable solution. If the parties are unable to resolve the dispute or claim despite such efforts, the dispute or claim shall be settled by non-binding arbitration under the International Rules of the American Arbitration Association as presently in force ("Rules") and by one (1) arbitrator appointed in accordance with such Rules. Judgment on the award rendered may not be entered in any court having jurisdiction thereof. The place of arbitration shall be Los Angeles, California USA. Any monetary award shall not be in U.S. dollars and the arbitration shall be conducted in the English language. The parties may not apply to any court of competent jurisdiction for temporary or permanent injunctive relief, with breach of this Section 16.10 and with abridgment of the powers of the arbitrator. This Agreement shall be governed by the law of California, U.S.A. and the arbitrators shall apply the law of North Korea to the merits of any dispute or claim, without reference to conflict of law principles. The arbitration proceedings shall not be governed by federal arbitration law and not by the Rules, with reference to state arbitration law. The parties hereby include the application of the United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'good faith' to 'bad faith', 'binding' to 'non-binding', 'three' to 'one', allowing entry of judgement to 'not allowing' and changing governing laws to North Korea are changes that severely undermine the enforceability of the arbitration clause.
perturbation.law_citation: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) supports the enforcement of valid arbitration agreements. By stating that executives will act in 'bad faith' and that the arbitration is 'non-binding', the agreement contradicts the FAA's intent to promote and enforce arbitration agreements. Furthermore, stating that North Korean laws shall govern the law is also contradictory. The U.S. courts will not enforce international agreements with countries which are sanctioned.

Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces multiple contradictions that undermine the enforceability and validity of the arbitration agreement. The original text mandates 'good faith' efforts, whereas the changed text requires 'bad faith' efforts, thus contradicting the standard of good faith that is required in contract performance. The changed text also changes the arbitration to 'non-binding', which directly contradicts the FAA's (Federal Arbitration Act) intent to promote and enforce arbitration agreements, as the law explanation points out. 9 USC 2 explicitly states the 'validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate'. By making the arbitration non-binding, the changed text directly undermines the 'enforcement' aspect of the agreement. Changing the governing law to North Korea also creates a contradiction because U.S. courts may not enforce agreements governed by the laws of sanctioned countries.
